title: papercheck
text: function study_from_xml() can read XML files TEI format created grobid.
code: grobid_dir <- system.file("grobid", package="papercheck") filename <- file.path(grobid_dir, "incest.pdf.tei.xml") s <- study_from_xml(filename) #> Processing incest.pdf.tei.xml...

title: papercheck
text: can access parsed table full text paper via s$full_text However, may find convenient use function search_full_text(). (Note grobid often bad parsing sentences statistics, break multiple sentences. working ways detecting fixing .) References omitted default, can add back .
code: dplyr::count(s$full_text, section_class) #>   section_class  n #> 1      abstract  6 #> 2         intro 25 #> 3        method 25 #> 4       results 16 #> 5    discussion 12 #> 6        figure  5 search_full_text(s, "significan[t|ce]") #>   section_class section div p s #> 1       results   5_div   5 2 1 #> 2       results   5_div   5 3 1 #> 3       results   5_div   5 3 7 #> 4    discussion   6_div   6 3 1 #>                                                                                                                                                                            text #> 1                      Our main, omnibus analysis showed a significant three-way interaction among incest type, participant sex, and sibling type (Estimate = 0.285, t(578.003) #> 2                Our analysis of moral wrongness scores for sibling incest scores showed a significant interaction between participant sex and sibling type (Estimate = -0.559, #> 3 In contrast, our analysis of moral wrongness scores for parental incest scores showed no significant interaction between participant sex and sibling type (Estimate = -0.268, #> 4                                                 In contrast, having other-sex siblings was not significantly associated with greater moral opposition to parent-child incest. #>                 file #> 1 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 2 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 3 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 4 incest.pdf.tei.xml search_full_text(s, "third-party", section = "intro", refs = TRUE) #>   section_class section div p s #> 1         intro   1_div   1 3 3 #> 2         intro   1_div   1 4 3 #> 3         intro   1_div   1 4 4 #> 4         intro   1_div   1 5 1 #> 5         intro   1_div   1 5 2 #>                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   text #> 1 This observation led Lieberman and colleagues to propose that moral opposition to third-party sibling incest may be greater among individuals with other-sex siblings than among individuals who do not have other-sex siblings (Lieberman et al., 2007 (Lieberman et al.,, 2002)) . #> 2                                                                                 Royzman et al's non-replication potentially calls into question the reliability of previously reported links between having an other-sex sibling and moral opposition to third-party sibling incest. #> 3                                                                      Royzman et al's (2008) non-replication also potentially calls into question the broader claim that moral opposition to third-party sibling incest is sensitive to contextual factors, such as family structure. #> 4                                                                                  In light of the above, we tested whether moral opposition to third-party sibling incest is greater among people who have only other-sex siblings than among people who have only same-sex siblings. #> 5                                                                                               We also investigated the specificity of this putative effect by testing whether moral opposition to third-party parent-child incest is similarly related to having other-sex siblings. #>                 file #> 1 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 2 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 3 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 4 incest.pdf.tei.xml #> 5 incest.pdf.tei.xml

title: papercheck
text: function study_from_xml() also works folder XML files, returning list scienceverse study objects, search_full_text() works list.
code: studies <- study_from_xml(grobid_dir) #> Processing eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml... #> Processing incest.pdf.tei.xml... #> Processing prereg.pdf.tei.xml...  search_full_text(studies, "hypothesi", section = "intro") #>                         section_class section div  p  s #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.1          intro   1_div   1  3  3 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.2          intro   1_div   1  4  2 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.3          intro   1_div   1  4  3 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.4          intro   1_div   1  4  5 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.5          intro   1_div   1  4 12 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.6          intro   1_div   1  4 14 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.7          intro   1_div   1  4 21 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.8          intro   1_div   1  4 22 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.9          intro   1_div   1  4 24 #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.10         intro   1_div   1  4 25 #> incest.pdf.tei.xml              intro   1_div   1  2  2 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.1            intro  01_div   1  1  3 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.2            intro  01_div   1  5  1 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.3            intro  02_div   2  2  5 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.4            intro  02_div   2  2  6 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.5            intro  02_div   2  4  8 #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.6            intro  03_div   3 11  7 #>                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 text #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.1  However, this hypothesis is difficult to reconcile with consistent findings that the parent--child relationship affects the strength of preferences for faces that resemble one's other--sex parent and the strength of resemblance between that parent and one's spouse, as well as husband--father resemblance being observed even among adopted women who were not genetically related to their fathers . #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.2                                                                                 While the matching hypothesis predicts that the best predictor of partner's eye color will be own eye color, the sex--linked heritable preference hypothesis predicts this will be the other--sex parent's eye color and the positive sexual imprinting hypothesis predicts this will be the partner--sex parent's eye color. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.3                                                                                                                                                            While these latter two predictions lead to the same pattern of results for heterosexual couples, here we also test same--sex couples to distinguish the sex-linked heritable preference hypothesis from the positive sexual imprinting hypothesis. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.4                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        The best model supported the positive sexual imprinting hypothesis, where maternal eye color significantly predicted the eye color of female partners (B = 0.812, S.E. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.5                                                                                                                                                                                                              This model was supported 7.455 times more strongly than the model corresponding to the matching hypothesis, where own eye color did not significantly predict partner eye color (B = 0.263, S.E. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.6                                                                                                                                                                  The imprinting model was also supported 9.655 times more strongly than the model corresponding to the sex--linked heritable preference hypothesis, where maternal eye color did not predict the eye color of men's partners (B = 0.262, S.E. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.7                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          The model supporting the positive sexual imprinting hypothesis remained the best model (see Table ). #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.8                                                                                                                                                                                                                         Our data provide clear evidence against the sex--linked heritable preferences hypothesis, which predicts a relationship between partner's eye color and other-sex parent's eye color. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.9                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           Our data also give little support to the matching hypothesis, as own eye color was positively but not significantly related to partner's eye color. #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.10                                                                                                                                                            Our data give clearest support to the positive sexual imprinting hypothesis, where we found that partner's eye color was predicted by maternal eye color for people with female partners and by paternal eye color for people with male partners. #> incest.pdf.tei.xml                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Consequently, humans (like many other animals) are hypothesized to possess adaptions to reduce inbreeding. #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.1                                                                                                                                      Clearly specifying the rules that will be used to terminate the data collection, and specifying the analysis plan before data is collected, can prevent undesirable research practices such as inflated of Type 1 errors and "hypothesizing after the results are known" . #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.2                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  The hypothesis that more efficient study designs might increase the willingness to pre-register rests on several requirements. #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.3                                                                                                                                                                                                                             respond to the statement that "One should always use two-sided p-values" with a resounding "No!", indicating that when a hypothesis is directional, a one-tailed test is required . #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.4                                                                                                                                         Since one-sided tests are only appropriate whenever null-hypothesis significance tests are appropriate, we have to assume that a null-hypothesis test answers a question a researcher is interested in, and that the null-hypothesis is both plausible and interesting. #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.5                                                                                                                                                                                                     The difference between hypothesis testing and estimation is relevant here -an unexpected effect might not confirm a hypothesis, but the descriptives related to the effect size might still be interesting. #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.6                                                                                              Purely descriptive studies where all data is reported might not require pre-registration, small pilot studies to test the feasibility of a paradigm are not designed to test a hypothesis, and some researchers might only publish studies when they have directly replicated every test in an independent sample. #>                                         file #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.1  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.2  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.3  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.4  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.5  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.6  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.7  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.8  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.9  eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml.10 eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml #> incest.pdf.tei.xml        incest.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.1      prereg.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.2      prereg.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.3      prereg.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.4      prereg.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.5      prereg.pdf.tei.xml #> prereg.pdf.tei.xml.6      prereg.pdf.tei.xml

text: function study_from_xml() can read XML files TEI format created grobid. example uses previously created XML files included papercheck. can use grobid paper .
code: grobid_dir <- system.file("grobid", package="papercheck") filename <- file.path(grobid_dir, "incest.pdf.tei.xml") s <- study_from_xml(filename) #> Processing incest.pdf.tei.xml...

title: Replace values if NULL, NA, blank after trimming, or an empty list — if_nowt
Replace values if NULL, NA, blank after trimming, or an empty list — if_nowt
text: x vector list test replace value replace test_for values test ("null" replaces NULL values, "na", replaces NA values, "trim" replaces empty strings trimws(), "empty" replaces empty lists)

title: Replace values if NULL, NA, blank after trimming, or an empty list — if_nowt
text: vector list replaced values

title: Replace values if NULL, NA, blank after trimming, or an empty list — if_nowt
code: if_nowt(NULL) #> [1] "" if_nowt(NA) #> [1] "" if_nowt("   ") #> [1] "" if_nowt(c(1, 2, NA), replace = 0) #> [1] 1 2 0 x <- list(NULL, NA, " ", list()) if_nowt(x) |> str() #> List of 4 #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : chr "" if_nowt(x, test_for = "null") |> str() #> List of 4 #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : logi NA #>  $ : chr " " #>  $ : list() if_nowt(x, test_for = "na") |> str() #> List of 4 #>  $ : NULL #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : chr " " #>  $ : list() if_nowt(x, test_for = "trim") |> str() #> List of 4 #>  $ : NULL #>  $ : logi NA #>  $ : chr "" #>  $ : list() if_nowt(x, test_for = "empty") |> str() #> List of 4 #>  $ : NULL #>  $ : logi NA #>  $ : chr " " #>  $ : chr ""

text: study study object created study_from_xml list study objects pattern regex pattern search section section(s) search return kind text return, full sentence, paragraph, section text , just (regex) match refs whether include references ignore.case whether ignore case text searching ... additional arguments pass grep() regexpr()

title: Search the full text — search_full_text
code: grobid_dir <- system.file("grobid", package="scienceverse") filename <- file.path(grobid_dir, "eyecolor.pdf.tei.xml") study <- study_from_xml(filename) #> Error in if (file.info(filename)$isdir) {    xmls <- list.files(filename, "\\.xml", full.names = TRUE)    if (length(xmls) == 0) {        stop("There are no xml files in the directory ", filename)    }    s <- study_from_xml(xmls)    names(s) <- basename(xmls)    return(s)}: missing value where TRUE/FALSE needed sig <- search_full_text(study, "significant", "results") #> Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'study' not found
